title: Dravidian peoples

infobox.value: or **Dravidians**, are a collection of [ethnolinguistic groups](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") native to [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") who speak [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages"). There are around 250 million native speakers of Dravidian languages.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The two largest Dravidian groups are the [Telugus](/wiki/Telugus "Telugus") (c. 90M) and [Tamils](/wiki/Tamilians "Tamilians") (c. 90M). The next three largest are the [Kannadigas](/wiki/Kannadigas "Kannadigas") (c. 44M), [Malayalis](/wiki/Malayalis "Malayalis") (c. 40M), and [Gondis](/wiki/Gondi_people "Gondi people") (c. 13M). India's [22 scheduled languages](/wiki/22_scheduled_languages "22 scheduled languages") include these four Dravidian languages: [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), and [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam").[[2]](#cite_note-SwanSmith2001-2) Dravidian speakers form the majority of the population of [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") and are native to [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"),[[3]](#cite_note-LouisSteever2015-3) [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"),[[4]](#cite_note-karim-4) [the Maldives](/wiki/The_Maldives "The Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). Dravidian peoples are also present in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") through [migration](/wiki/South_Indian_diaspora "South Indian diaspora").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Kalaripayattu_mock_combat_in_rural_Kerala.jpg/250px-Kalaripayattu_mock_combat_in_rural_Kerala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kalaripayattu_mock_combat_in_rural_Kerala.jpg)

Dravidian men performing the [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") Dravidian native martial art, [Kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu").

The **Dravidian peoples**, **Dravidian-speakers** or **Dravidians**, are a collection of [ethnolinguistic groups](/wiki/Ethnolinguistic_group "Ethnolinguistic group") native to [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") who speak [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages"). There are around 250 million native speakers of Dravidian languages.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The two largest Dravidian groups are the [Telugus](/wiki/Telugus "Telugus") (c. 90M) and [Tamils](/wiki/Tamilians "Tamilians") (c. 90M). The next three largest are the [Kannadigas](/wiki/Kannadigas "Kannadigas") (c. 44M), [Malayalis](/wiki/Malayalis "Malayalis") (c. 40M), and [Gondis](/wiki/Gondi_people "Gondi people") (c. 13M). India's [22 scheduled languages](/wiki/22_scheduled_languages "22 scheduled languages") include these four Dravidian languages: [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada"), and [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam").[[2]](#cite_note-SwanSmith2001-2) Dravidian speakers form the majority of the population of [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") and are native to [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"),[[3]](#cite_note-LouisSteever2015-3) [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"),[[4]](#cite_note-karim-4) [the Maldives](/wiki/The_Maldives "The Maldives"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan")[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). Dravidian peoples are also present in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa"), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") through [migration](/wiki/South_Indian_diaspora "South Indian diaspora").

[Proto-Dravidian](/wiki/Proto-Dravidian_language "Proto-Dravidian language") may have been spoken in the Indus civilization, suggesting a "tentative date of Proto-Dravidian around the early part of the third millennium BCE",[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003501-6) after which it branched into various Dravidian languages.[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7) South Dravidian I (including pre-[Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language")) and South Dravidian II (including pre-[Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language")) split around the eleventh century BCE, with the other major branches splitting off at around the same time.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003501–502-8)

The origins of the Dravidians are a "very complex subject of research and debate".[[9]](#cite_note-Tudu2008_p.4002-9) They are regarded as indigenous to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"),[[10]](#cite_note-Avari_p132-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Masica_p392-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Kopstein_p3452-12) but may have deeper pre-Neolithic roots from [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia"), specifically from the [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavalli-SforzaMenozziPiazza1994221–222-13)[[14]](#cite_note-kumar20042-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKivisild19991333-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola201517-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17) Their origins are often viewed as being connected with the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation"),[[9]](#cite_note-Tudu2008_p.4002-9)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola2015-18) hence people and language spread east and southwards after the demise of the Indus Valley Civilisation in the early second millennium BCE,[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.201815-19)[[20]](#cite_note-scientificamerican.com2-20) some propose not long before the arrival of Indo-Aryan speakers,[[21]](#cite_note-The_Dravidianization_of_India2-21) with whom they intensively interacted.[[22]](#cite_note-britannicaOnline2-22) Some scholars have argued that the Dravidian languages may have been brought to India by migrations from the Iranian plateau in the fourth or third millennium BCE[[23]](#cite_note-tls1963-23)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndronov2003299-24) or even earlier.[[25]](#cite_note-mukherjee20012-25)[[26]](#cite_note-kumar20043-26) However, reconstructed proto-Dravidian vocabulary suggests that the family is indigenous to India.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti200315-27)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvari200713-28)

Genetically, the ancient Indus Valley people were composed of a primarily Iranian hunter-gatherers (or farmers) ancestry, with varying degrees of ancestry from local hunter-gatherer groups. The modern-day Dravidian-speakers are primarily composed of Ancient South Indian hunter-gatherer ancestry and varying levels of Indus Valley Civilisation ancestry, but also carry a small portion of [Western Steppe Herder](/wiki/Western_Steppe_Herders "Western Steppe Herders") ancestry and may also have additional contributions from local hunter-gatherer groups.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReich_et_al.2009-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.2019-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The third century BCE onwards saw the development of many great empires in South India like [Pandya](/wiki/Pandya "Pandya"), [Chola](/wiki/Chola "Chola"), [Chera](/wiki/Chera "Chera"), [Pallava](/wiki/Pallava "Pallava"), [Satavahana](/wiki/Satavahana "Satavahana"), [Chalukya](/wiki/Chalukya "Chalukya"), [Kakatiya](/wiki/Kakatiya "Kakatiya") and [Rashtrakuta](/wiki/Rashtrakuta "Rashtrakuta"). Medieval South Indian guilds and trading organisations like the "Ayyavole of Karnataka and Manigramam" played an important role in the Southeast Asia trade,[[32]](#cite_note-World_p.293-32) and the [cultural Indianisation](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India") of the region.

Dravidian visual art is dominated by [stylised temple architecture](/wiki/Dravidian_architecture "Dravidian architecture") in major centres, and the production of images on stone and bronze sculptures. The sculpture dating from the [Chola period](/wiki/Chola_art "Chola art") has become notable as a symbol of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"). The [Sri Ranganathaswamy Temple](/wiki/Ranganathaswamy_Temple,_Srirangam "Ranganathaswamy Temple, Srirangam") located in Indian state of [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") is often considered as the largest functioning Hindu temple in the world. The temple is built in Dravidian style and occupies an area of 156 acres (631,000 m2).[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Etymology

Main article: [Dravidian languages § Etymology](/wiki/Dravidian_languages#Etymology "Dravidian languages")

The origin of the Sanskrit word **drāviḍa** is [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language").[[34]](#cite_note-34) In [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit"), words such as "Damela", "Dameda", "Dhamila" and "Damila", which later evolved from "Tamila", could have been used to denote an ethnic identity.[[35]](#cite_note-KI157-35) In the Sanskrit tradition, the word **drāviḍa** was also used to denote the geographical region of South India.[[36]](#cite_note-Zvelebil_1990_xx-36) Epigraphic evidence of an ethnic group termed as such is found in ancient India and Sri Lanka where a number of inscriptions have come to light datable from the 2nd century BCE mentioning *Damela* or *Dameda* persons.[[35]](#cite_note-KI157-35) The [Hathigumpha inscription](/wiki/Hathigumpha_inscription "Hathigumpha inscription") of the [Kalinga](/wiki/Kalinga_(historical_kingdom) "Kalinga (historical kingdom)") ruler [Kharavela](/wiki/Kharavela "Kharavela") refers to a *T(ra)mira samghata* (Confederacy of Tamil rulers) dated to 150 BCE. It also mentions that the league of Tamil kingdoms had been in existence for 113 years by that time.[[35]](#cite_note-KI157-35) In [Amaravati](/wiki/Amaravathi_village,_Guntur_district "Amaravathi village, Guntur district") in present-day [Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Andhra_Pradesh "Andhra Pradesh") there is an inscription referring to a *Dhamila-vaniya* (Tamil trader) datable to the 3rd century CE.[[35]](#cite_note-KI157-35) Another inscription of about the same time in [Nagarjunakonda](/wiki/Nagarjunakonda "Nagarjunakonda") seems to refer to a *Damila*. A third inscription in [Kanheri Caves](/wiki/Kanheri_Caves "Kanheri Caves") refers to a *Dhamila-gharini* (Tamil householder). In the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") [Jataka](/wiki/Jataka "Jataka") story known as *Akiti Jataka* there is a mention to *Damila-rattha* (Tamil dynasty).

While the English word *Dravidian* was first employed by [Robert Caldwell](/wiki/Robert_Caldwell "Robert Caldwell") in his book of comparative Dravidian grammar based on the usage of the Sanskrit word **drāviḍa** in the work *Tantravārttika* by *Kumārila Bhaṭṭa*,[[36]](#cite_note-Zvelebil_1990_xx-36) the word **drāviḍa** in Sanskrit has been historically used to denote geographical regions of southern India as whole. Some theories concern the direction of derivation between **tamiḻ** and **drāviḍa**; such linguists as Zvelebil assert that the direction is from **tamiḻ** to **drāviḍa**.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Language

Main article: [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/DravidianTree.png/250px-DravidianTree.png)](/wiki/File:DravidianTree.png)

Dravidian language tree

The Dravidian language family is one of the oldest in the world. Six languages are currently recognized by India as Classical languages and four of them are Dravidian languages Tamil, Telugu, Kannada and Malayalam.

The most commonly spoken Dravidian languages are [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") (తెలుగు), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") (தமிழ்), [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language") (ಕನ್ನಡ), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_language "Malayalam language") (മലയാളം), [Brahui](/wiki/Brahui_language "Brahui language") (براہوئی), [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_Language "Tulu Language") (തുളു), [Gondi](/wiki/Gondi_language "Gondi language") and [Coorg](/wiki/Kodava_language "Kodava language"). There are three subgroups within the Dravidian language family: North Dravidian, Central Dravidian, and South Dravidian, matching for the most part the corresponding regions in the Indian subcontinent.

Dravidian grammatical impact on the structure and syntax of Indo-Aryan languages is considered far greater than the Indo-Aryan grammatical impact on Dravidian. Some linguists explain this anomaly by arguing that Middle Indo-Aryan and New Indo-Aryan were built on a Dravidian [substratum](/wiki/Substrata_(linguistics) "Substrata (linguistics)").[[45]](#cite_note-45) There are also hundreds of Dravidian loanwords in Indo-Aryan languages, and vice versa.

According to David McAlpin and his [Elamo-Dravidian hypothesis](/wiki/Elamo-Dravidian_languages "Elamo-Dravidian languages"), the Dravidian languages were brought to India by immigration into India from [Elam](/wiki/Elam "Elam"), located in present-day southwestern [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran").[[46]](#cite_note-kumar2004-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) In the 1990s, Renfrew and Cavalli-Sforza have also argued that Proto-Dravidian was brought to India by farmers from the Iranian part of the Fertile Crescent,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavalli-SforzaMenozziPiazza1994221–222-13)[[48]](#cite_note-mukherjee2001-48)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDerenko2013-49)[[note 1]](#cite_note-50) but more recently Heggerty and Renfrew noted that "McAlpin's analysis of the language data, and thus his claims, remain far from orthodoxy", adding that Fuller finds no relation of Dravidian language with other languages, and thus assumes it to be native to India.[[50]](#cite_note-Heggarty_Renfrew-51) Renfrew and Bahn conclude that several scenarios are compatible with the data, and that "the linguistic jury is still very much out."[[50]](#cite_note-Heggarty_Renfrew-51)

As a [proto-language](/wiki/Proto-language "Proto-language"), the [Proto-Dravidian](/wiki/Proto-Dravidian "Proto-Dravidian") language is not itself attested in the historical record. Its modern conception is based solely on reconstruction. It is suggested that the language was spoken in the 4th millennium BCE, and started disintegrating into various branches around 3rd millennium BCE.[[7]](#cite_note-books.google.com-7) According to Krishnamurti, Proto-Dravidian may have been spoken in the Indus civilisation, suggesting a "tentative date of Proto-Dravidian around the early part of the third millennium."[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003501-6) Krishnamurti further states that South Dravidian I (including pre-Tamil) and South Dravidian II (including pre-Telugu) split around the eleventh century BCE, with the other major branches splitting off at around the same time.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti2003501–502-8)

## History

### Origins

See also: [History of Dravidian languages](/wiki/Peopling_of_India "Peopling of India"), [Proto-Dravidian](/wiki/Genetics_and_archaeogenetics_of_South_Asia "Genetics and archaeogenetics of South Asia"), [Dravidian homeland](/wiki/Dravidian_languages#Prehistory "Dravidian languages"), [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Proto-Dravidian "Proto-Dravidian"), [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Dravidian_homeland "Dravidian homeland"), [Demic diffusion](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution"), [Origins of Mehrgahr](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent"), [Demic diffusion](/wiki/Demic_diffusion "Demic diffusion"), and [Mehrgarh § Origins](/wiki/Mehrgarh#Origins "Mehrgarh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Dancing_girl.jpg/250px-Dancing_girl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dancing_girl.jpg)

The [Dancing Girl](/wiki/Dancing_Girl_(prehistoric_sculpture) "Dancing Girl (prehistoric sculpture)"), a prehistoric [bronze sculpture](/wiki/Bronze_sculpture "Bronze sculpture") made in approximately 2500 BCE in the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") city of [Mohenjo-daro](/wiki/Mohenjo-daro "Mohenjo-daro").

The origins of the Dravidians are a "very complex subject of research and debate".[[9]](#cite_note-Tudu2008_p.4002-9) They are regarded as indigenous to the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"),[[10]](#cite_note-Avari_p132-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Masica_p392-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Kopstein_p3452-12) but may have deeper pre-Neolithic roots from [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia"), specifically from the [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECavalli-SforzaMenozziPiazza1994221–222-13)[[14]](#cite_note-kumar20042-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKivisild19991333-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola201517-16)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17) Their origins are often viewed as being connected with the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation"),[[9]](#cite_note-Tudu2008_p.4002-9)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola2015-18) hence people and language spread east and southwards after the demise of the Indus Valley Civilisation in the early second millennium BCE,[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.201815-19)[[20]](#cite_note-scientificamerican.com2-20) some propose not long before the arrival of Indo-Aryan speakers,[[21]](#cite_note-The_Dravidianization_of_India2-21) with whom they intensively interacted.[[22]](#cite_note-britannicaOnline2-22) Though some scholars have argued that the Dravidian languages may have been brought to India by migrations from the Iranian plateau in the fourth or third millennium BCE[[23]](#cite_note-tls1963-23)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndronov2003299-24) or even earlier,[[25]](#cite_note-mukherjee20012-25)[[26]](#cite_note-kumar20043-26) reconstructed proto-Dravidian vocabulary suggests that the family is indigenous to India.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKrishnamurti200315-27)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAvari200713-28)

Genetically, the ancient Indus Valley people were composed of a primarily Iranian hunter-gatherers (or farmers) ancestry, with varying degrees of ancestry from local hunter-gatherer groups. The modern-day Dravidian-speakers display a similar genetic makeup, but also carry a small portion of [Western Steppe Herder](/wiki/Western_Steppe_Herders "Western Steppe Herders") ancestry and may also have additional contributions from local hunter-gatherer groups.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReich_et_al.2009-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.2019-30)[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Although in modern times speakers of various [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_language "Dravidian language") have mainly occupied the southern portion of India, Dravidian speakers must have been widespread throughout the Indian subcontinent before the [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples") migration into the subcontinent.[[52]](#cite_note-britannicaOnline-53) According to Horen Tudu, "many academic researchers have attempted to connect the Dravidians with the remnants of the great [Indus Valley civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilisation "Indus Valley civilisation"), located in Northwestern India... but [i]t is mere speculation that the Dravidians are the ensuing post–Indus Valley settlement of refugees into South and Central India."[[53]](#cite_note-Tudu2008_p.400-54) The most noteworthy scholar making such claims is [Asko Parpola](/wiki/Asko_Parpola "Asko Parpola"),[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17) who did extensive research on the IVC-scripts.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESamuel200854_note_15-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola2015-18) The [Brahui](/wiki/Brahui_people "Brahui people") population of [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan_(Pakistan) "Balochistan (Pakistan)") in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") has been taken by some as the linguistic equivalent of a [relict](/wiki/Relict "Relict") population, perhaps indicating that Dravidian languages were formerly much more widespread and were supplanted by the incoming Indo-Aryan languages.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Asko Parpola, who regards the Harappans to have been Dravidian, notes that [Mehrgarh](/wiki/Mehrgarh "Mehrgarh") (7000–2500 BCE), to the west of the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") valley,[[55]](#cite_note-56) is a precursor of the Indus Valley Civilisation, whose inhabitants migrated into the Indus Valley and became the Indus Valley Civilisation.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola201517-16) It is one of the earliest sites with evidence of farming and herding in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia").[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) According to Lukacs and Hemphill, while there is a strong continuity between the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") and [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") (Copper Age) cultures of Mehrgarh, dental evidence shows that the Chalcolithic population did not descend from the Neolithic population of Mehrgarh,[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConinghamYoung2015114-59) which "suggests moderate levels of gene flow".[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConinghamYoung2015114-59) They further noted that "the direct lineal descendants of the Neolithic inhabitants of Mehrgarh are to be found to the south and the east of Mehrgarh, in northwestern India and the western edge of the Deccan plateau", with Neolithic Mehrgarh showing greater affinity with chalocolithic [Inamgaon](/wiki/Inamgaon "Inamgaon"), south of Mehrgarh, than with Chalcolithic Mehrgarh.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConinghamYoung2015114-59)

#### Indus Valley Civilization

Main articles: [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") and [Substratum in Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Substratum_in_Vedic_Sanskrit "Substratum in Vedic Sanskrit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Shiva_Pashupati.jpg/250px-Shiva_Pashupati.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shiva_Pashupati.jpg)

The *Pashupati* seal from the Indus Valley Civilization

##### Dravidian identification

The [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") (2,600–1,900 BCE) located in the northwest of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") is sometimes identified as having been Dravidian.[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-Ansumali_Mukhopadhyay_1–14-61) Already in 1924, when announcing the discovery of the IVC, [John Marshall](/wiki/John_Marshall_(archaeologist) "John Marshall (archaeologist)") stated that (one of) the language(s) may have been Dravidic.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Cultural and linguistic similarities have been cited by researchers [Henry Heras](/wiki/Henry_Heras "Henry Heras"), [Kamil Zvelebil](/wiki/Kamil_Zvelebil "Kamil Zvelebil"), [Asko Parpola](/wiki/Asko_Parpola "Asko Parpola") and [Iravatham Mahadevan](/wiki/Iravatham_Mahadevan "Iravatham Mahadevan") as being strong evidence for a proto-Dravidian origin of the ancient Indus Valley civilisation.[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) The discovery in Tamil Nadu of a late Neolithic (early 2nd millennium BCE, i.e. post-dating Harappan decline) stone [celt](/wiki/Celt_(tool) "Celt (tool)") allegedly marked with Indus signs has been considered by some to be significant for the Dravidian identification.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

[Yuri Knorozov](/wiki/Yuri_Knorozov "Yuri Knorozov") surmised that the symbols represent a [logosyllabic](/wiki/Logosyllabic "Logosyllabic") script and suggested, based on computer analysis, an agglutinative [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") language as the most likely candidate for the underlying language.[[66]](#cite_note-67) Knorozov's suggestion was preceded by the work of Henry Heras, who suggested several readings of signs based on a proto-Dravidian assumption.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

Linguist Asko Parpola writes that the Indus script and Harappan language are "most likely to have belonged to the Dravidian family".[[68]](#cite_note-69) Parpola led a Finnish team in investigating the inscriptions using computer analysis. Based on a proto-Dravidian assumption, they proposed readings of many signs, some agreeing with the suggested readings of Heras and Knorozov (such as equating the "fish" sign with the Dravidian word for fish, "min") but disagreeing on several other readings. A comprehensive description of Parpola's work until 1994 is given in his book *Deciphering the Indus Script*.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

##### Decline, migration and Dravidianization

[Paleoclimatologists](/wiki/Paleoclimatology "Paleoclimatology") believe the fall of the Indus Valley Civilisation and eastward migration during the late Harappan period was due to climate change in the region, with a 200-year long drought being the major factor.[[70]](#cite_note-scientificamerican.com-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73) The Indus Valley Civilisation seemed to slowly lose their urban cohesion, and their cities were gradually abandoned during the late Harappan period, followed by eastward migrations before the Indo-Aryan migration into the Indian subcontinent.[[70]](#cite_note-scientificamerican.com-71)

The process of post-Harappan/Dravidian influences on southern India has tentatively been called "Dravidianization",[[73]](#cite_note-The_Dravidianization_of_India-74) and is reflected in the post-Harappan mixture of IVC and Ancient Ancestral South Indian people.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENarasimhan_et_al.2018-75) Yet, according to Krishnamurti, Dravidian languages may have reached south India before Indo-Aryan migrations.[[52]](#cite_note-britannicaOnline-53)

#### Dravidian and Indo-Aryan interactions

##### Dravidian substrate

The Dravidian language influenced the Indo-Aryan languages. Dravidian languages show extensive lexical (vocabulary) borrowing, but only a few traits of structural (either [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") or grammatical) borrowing from Indo-Aryan, whereas Indo-Aryan shows more structural than lexical borrowings from the Dravidian languages.[[52]](#cite_note-britannicaOnline-53) Many of these features are already present in the oldest known [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_language "Indo-Aryan language"), the language of the *[Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda")* (c. 1500 BCE), which also includes over a dozen words borrowed from Dravidian. The linguistic evidence for Dravidian impact grows stronger as we move from the Samhitas down through the later Vedic works and into the classical post-Vedic literature.[[75]](#cite_note-Krishnamurti6-76) This represents an early religious and cultural fusion[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200750-77)[[note 2]](#cite_note-Lockard-79) or synthesis[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiltebeitel200712-80) between ancient Dravidians and Indo-Aryans.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200752-78)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETiwari2002v-81)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1951218–219-82)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarson199581-83)

According to Mallory there are an estimated thirty to forty Dravidian loanwords in [Rig Veda](/wiki/Rig_Veda "Rig Veda").[[82]](#cite_note-MalloryAdams-84) Some of those for which Dravidian etymologies are certain include ಕುಲಾಯ *kulāya* "nest", ಕುಲ್ಫ *kulpha* "ankle", ದಂಡ **daṇḍa** "stick", ಕುಲ *kūla* "slope", ಬಿಲ *bila* "hollow", ಖಲ *khala* "threshing floor".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZvelebil199081-85) While J. Bloch and [M. Witzel](/wiki/Michael_Witzel "Michael Witzel") believe that the Indo-Aryans moved into an already Dravidian-speaking area after the oldest parts of the [Rig Veda](/wiki/Rig_Veda "Rig Veda") were already composed.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

According to Thomason and Kaufman, there is strong evidence that Dravidian influenced [Indic](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") through "shift", that is, native Dravidian speakers learning and adopting Indic languages.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomasonKaufman1988-87) According to Erdosy, the most plausible explanation for the presence of Dravidian structural features in Old Indo-Aryan is that the majority of early Old Indo-Aryan speakers had a Dravidian mother tongue which they gradually abandoned. [Erdosy (1995](#CITEREFErdosy1995):18) Even though the innovative traits in Indic could be explained by multiple internal explanations, early Dravidian influence is the only explanation that can account for all of the innovations at once. Early Dravidian influence accounts for several of the innovative traits in Indic better than any internal explanation that has been proposed.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomasonKaufman1988141–144-88) According to Zvelebil, "several scholars have demonstrated that pre-Indo-Aryan and pre-Dravidian bilingualism in India provided conditions for the far-reaching influence of Dravidian on the Indo-Aryan tongues in the spheres of phonology, syntax and vocabulary."[[87]](#cite_note-89)

##### Sanskritization

With the rise of the [Kuru kingdom](/wiki/Kuru_kingdom "Kuru kingdom") a process of [Sanskritization](/wiki/Sanskritization "Sanskritization") started which influenced all of India, with the populations of the north of the Indian subcontinent predominantly speaking the Indo-Aryan languages.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel1995-90)

### Dravidian kingdoms and empires

The third century BCE onwards saw the development of large Dravidian empires like [Chola](/wiki/Chola_Empire "Chola Empire"), [Pandya](/wiki/Pandyan_dynasty "Pandyan dynasty"), [Rashtrakuta](/wiki/Rashtrakuta_dynasty "Rashtrakuta dynasty"), [Vijayanagara](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire"), [Chalukyas](/wiki/Chalukya_dynasty "Chalukya dynasty") [Western Chalukya](/wiki/Western_Chalukya_Empire "Western Chalukya Empire"), and kingdoms like [Chera](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty"), [Chutu](/wiki/Chutu_dynasty "Chutu dynasty"), [Ay](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom"), [Alupa](/wiki/Alupa_dynasty "Alupa dynasty"), [Pallava](/wiki/Pallava_dynasty "Pallava dynasty"), [Hoysala](/wiki/Hoysala_Empire "Hoysala Empire"), [Western Ganga](/wiki/Western_Ganga_dynasty "Western Ganga dynasty"), [Eastern Ganga](/wiki/Eastern_Ganga_dynasty "Eastern Ganga dynasty"), [Kadamba](/wiki/Kadamba_dynasty "Kadamba dynasty"), [Kalabhra](/wiki/Kalabhra_dynasty "Kalabhra dynasty"), [Andhra Ikshvaku](/wiki/Andhra_Ikshvaku "Andhra Ikshvaku"), [Vishnukundina](/wiki/Vishnukundina "Vishnukundina"), [Eastern Chalukya](/wiki/Eastern_Chalukya "Eastern Chalukya"), [Sena](/wiki/Sena_dynasty "Sena dynasty"), [Kakatiya](/wiki/Kakatiya_dynasty "Kakatiya dynasty"), [Reddy](/wiki/Reddy_dynasty "Reddy dynasty"), [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore_kingdom "Mysore kingdom"), [Jaffna](/wiki/Jaffna_kingdom "Jaffna kingdom"), [Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore"), [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore"), [Venad](/wiki/Venad "Venad"), [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin"), [Cannanore](/wiki/Kolathunadu "Kolathunadu"), [Calicut](/wiki/Zamorin_of_Calicut "Zamorin of Calicut") and the [Nayakas](/wiki/Nayak_dynasty "Nayak dynasty").

### Medieval trade and influence

Medieval Tamil guilds and trading organisations like the Ayyavole and [Manigramam](/wiki/Manigramam "Manigramam") played an important role in the southeast Asia trade.[[32]](#cite_note-World_p.293-32) Traders and religious leaders travelled to southeast Asia and played an important role in the [cultural Indianisation](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India") of the region. Locally developed scripts such as [Grantha](/wiki/Grantha_script "Grantha script") and [Pallava script](/wiki/Pallava_script "Pallava script") induced the development of many native scripts such as [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_script "Khmer script"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_script "Javanese script") [Kawi](/wiki/Kawi_script "Kawi script"), [Baybayin](/wiki/Baybayin "Baybayin"), and [Thai](/wiki/Thai_script "Thai script").

Around this time, Dravidians encountered Muslim traders, and the first [Tamil Muslims](/wiki/Tamil_Muslim "Tamil Muslim") and [Sri Lankan Moors](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Moors "Sri Lankan Moors") appeared.

### European contact (1500 onward)

Portuguese explorers like Vasco de Gama were motivated to expand mainly for the spice markets of Calicut (today called Kozhikode) in modern-day Kerala. This led to the establishment of a series of Portuguese colonies along the western coasts of Karnataka and Kerala, including Mangalore. During this time Portuguese Jesuit priests also arrived and converted a small number of people in modern Kerala, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu to Catholicism, most notably the Paravars.

## Dravidian culture

### Religious belief

See also: [Dravidian folk religion](/wiki/Dravidian_folk_religion "Dravidian folk religion"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa"), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Charvaka](/wiki/Charvaka "Charvaka"), [Ājīvika](/wiki/%C4%80j%C4%ABvika "Ājīvika"), and [Indus Valley Civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilization "Indus Valley Civilization")

Ancient Dravidian religion constituted of an [animistic](/wiki/Animism "Animism") and non-[Vedic](/wiki/Historical_Vedic_religion "Historical Vedic religion") form of religion which may have influenced the [Āgamas](/wiki/%C4%80gama_(Hinduism) "Āgama (Hinduism)"), Vedic and non-[Vedic](/wiki/Vedic "Vedic") texts[[89]](#cite_note-Narasimhachary-91) which post-date the Vedic texts.[[90]](#cite_note-Tripath2001-92) The *Agamas* are [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") [scriptures](/wiki/Religious_text "Religious text") chiefly constituting the methods of temple construction and creation of *[murti](/wiki/Murti "Murti")*, worship means of deities, philosophical doctrines, meditative practices, attainment of sixfold desires and four kinds of yoga.[[91]](#cite_note-Grimes1996-93) The worship of [village deities](/wiki/Village_deities_of_Tamil_Nadu "Village deities of Tamil Nadu"), as well as sacred flora and fauna in Hinduism is recognised as a survival of the pre-Vedic Dravidian religion.[[92]](#cite_note-94) Hinduism can be regarded as a religious and cultural fusion[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200750-77)[[note 2]](#cite_note-Lockard-79) or synthesis[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiltebeitel200712-80) between ancient Dravidians and Indo-Aryans, and other local elements.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200752-78)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETiwari2002v-81)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1951218–219-82)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarson199581-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/WLA_lacma_12th_century_Maharishi_Agastya.jpg/250px-WLA_lacma_12th_century_Maharishi_Agastya.jpg)](/wiki/File:WLA_lacma_12th_century_Maharishi_Agastya.jpg)

Sage [Agastya](/wiki/Agastya "Agastya"), father of Tamil literature

Ancient Tamil grammatical works [Tolkappiyam](/wiki/Tolkappiyam "Tolkappiyam"), the ten anthologies [Pattuppāṭṭu](/wiki/Pattupp%C4%81%E1%B9%AD%E1%B9%ADu "Pattuppāṭṭu"), and the eight anthologies [Eṭṭuttokai](/wiki/E%E1%B9%AD%E1%B9%ADuttokai "Eṭṭuttokai") shed light on early ancient Dravidian religion. [Murugan](/wiki/Murugan "Murugan") (also known as Seyyon) was glorified as *the red god seated on the blue peacock, who is ever young and resplendent*, as *the favoured god of the Tamils*.[[93]](#cite_note-Kanchan_Sinha_1979-95) [Sivan](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") was also seen as the supreme God.[[93]](#cite_note-Kanchan_Sinha_1979-95) Early iconography of [Murugan](/wiki/Murugan "Murugan")[[94]](#cite_note-96) and [Sivan](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva")[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99) and their association with native flora and fauna goes back to the Indus Valley Civilisation.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101) The [Sangam landscape](/wiki/Sangam_landscape "Sangam landscape") was classified into five categories, *thinais*, based on the mood, the season and the land. Tolkappiyam mentions that each of these *thinai* had an associated deity such as Seyyon in *Kurinji* (hills), [Thirumaal](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu") in *Mullai* (forests), and [Kotravai](/wiki/Durga "Durga") in *Marutham* (plains), and [Wanji-ko](/wiki/Indra "Indra") in the *Neithal* (coasts and seas). Other gods mentioned were [Mayyon](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") and [Vaali](/wiki/Balaram "Balaram"), now identified with Krishna and Balarama, who are all major deities in Hinduism today. This represents an early religious and cultural fusion[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200750-77)[[note 2]](#cite_note-Lockard-79) or synthesis[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHiltebeitel200712-80) between ancient Dravidians and Indo-Aryans, which became more evident over time with sacred iconography, traditions, philosophy, flora and fauna that went on to influence and shape Indian civilisation.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETiwari2002v-81)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELockard200752-78)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmer1951218–219-82)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELarson199581-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Madurai_The_City_of_Temples.jpg/250px-Madurai_The_City_of_Temples.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madurai_The_City_of_Temples.jpg)

[Meenakshi Amman temple](/wiki/Meenakshi_Amman_temple "Meenakshi Amman temple"), dedicated to Goddess [Meenakshi](/wiki/Meenakshi "Meenakshi"), tutelary deity of Madurai city

Throughout [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam"), a king was considered to be divine by nature and possessed religious significance.[[100]](#cite_note-Harman_1992_6-102) The king was 'the representative of God on earth' and lived in a "koyil", which means the "residence of a god". The Modern Tamil word for temple is *[koil](/wiki/Koil "Koil")* ([Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"): கோயில்). Ritual worship was also given to kings.[[101]](#cite_note-Anand_1980-103)[[102]](#cite_note-Chopra_1979-104) Modern words for god like "kō" ([Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"): கோ "king"), "iṟai" (இறை "emperor") and "āṇḍavar" (ஆண்டவன் "conqueror") now primarily refer to gods. These elements were incorporated later into Hinduism like the legendary marriage of [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") to Queen Mīnātchi who ruled [Madurai](/wiki/Madurai "Madurai") or [Wanji-ko](/wiki/Legendary_early_Chola_kings#Sangam_period_Chola_rulers "Legendary early Chola kings"), a god who later merged into [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra").[[103]](#cite_note-Bate_2009-105) Tolkappiyar refers to the [Three Crowned Kings](/wiki/Three_Crowned_Kings "Three Crowned Kings") as the "Three Glorified by Heaven", ([Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"): வாண்புகழ் மூவர், *Vāṉpukaḻ Mūvar*).[[104]](#cite_note-A._Kiruṭṭin̲an̲_2000_17-106) In Dravidian-speaking South India, the concept of divine kingship led to the assumption of major roles by state and temple.[[105]](#cite_note-Embree_1988-107)

The cult of the mother goddess is treated as an indication of a society which venerated femininity. This mother goddess was conceived as a virgin, one who has given birth to all and one, and were typically associated with [Shaktism](/wiki/History_of_Shaktism "History of Shaktism").[[106]](#cite_note-108) The temples of the Sangam days, mainly of Madurai, seem to have had priestesses to the deity, which also appears predominantly as a goddess.[[107]](#cite_note-109) In the Sangam literature, there is an elaborate description of the rites performed by the Kurava priestess in the shrine Palamutircholai.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

Among the early Dravidians, the practice of erecting memorial stones, [Natukal and Viragal](/wiki/Hero_stone "Hero stone"), had appeared, and it continued for quite a long time after the Sangam age, down to about the 16th century.[[109]](#cite_note-shashi1996-111) It was customary for people who sought victory in war to worship these [hero stones](/wiki/Hero_stone "Hero stone") to bless them with victory*.[[110]](#cite_note-subramanium1980-112)*

### Architecture and visual art

Main article: [Dravidian Architecture](/wiki/Dravidian_Architecture "Dravidian Architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Nataraja01.jpg/250px-Nataraja01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nataraja01.jpg)

[Nataraja](/wiki/Nataraja "Nataraja"), example of [Chola Empire](/wiki/Chola_Empire "Chola Empire") bronze has become notable as a symbol of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/01AnnamalaiyarTemple%26Thiruvannamalai%26TamilNadu%26AerialViewfromVirupakshaCave.jpg/250px-01AnnamalaiyarTemple%26Thiruvannamalai%26TamilNadu%26AerialViewfromVirupakshaCave.jpg)](/wiki/File:01AnnamalaiyarTemple%26Thiruvannamalai%26TamilNadu%26AerialViewfromVirupakshaCave.jpg)

Typical layout of Dravidian temple architecture, 9th century A.D

*Mayamata* and *Manasara shilpa* texts estimated to be in circulation by the 5th to 7th century AD, are guidebooks on the Dravidian style of [Vastu Shastra](/wiki/Vastu_Shastra "Vastu Shastra") design, construction, sculpture and joinery technique.[[111]](#cite_note-stellakramrisch76-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114) *Isanasivagurudeva paddhati* is another text from the 9th century describing the art of building in India in south and central India.[[111]](#cite_note-stellakramrisch76-113)[[113]](#cite_note-115) In north India, *Brihat-samhita* by [Varāhamihira](/wiki/Var%C4%81hamihira "Varāhamihira") is the widely cited ancient Sanskrit manual from the 6th century describing the design and construction of *Nagara*-style Hindu temples.[[114]](#cite_note-mmgeometry-116)[[115]](#cite_note-hgood-117)[[116]](#cite_note-118) Traditional Dravidian architecture and symbolism are also based on Agamas. The Agamas are non-[Vedic](/wiki/Vedic "Vedic") in origin[[89]](#cite_note-Narasimhachary-91) and have been dated either as post-Vedic texts[[90]](#cite_note-Tripath2001-92) or as pre-Vedic compositions.[[117]](#cite_note-Nagalingam-119) The *Agamas* are a collection of Tamil and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") [scriptures](/wiki/Religious_text "Religious text") chiefly constituting the methods of temple construction and creation of *[murti](/wiki/Murti "Murti")*, worship means of deities, philosophical doctrines, meditative practices, attainment of sixfold desires and four kinds of yoga.[[91]](#cite_note-Grimes1996-93)

Chola-style temples consist almost invariably of the three following parts, arranged in differing manners, but differing in themselves only according to the age in which they were executed:[[118]](#cite_note-Fergusson-120)

1. The porches or *Mantapas*, which always cover and precede the door leading to the cell.
2. Gate-pyramids, *Gopuras*, which are the principal features in the quadrangular enclosures that surround the more notable temples. Gopuras are very common in Dravidian temples.
3. Pillared halls (*Chaultris* or *Chawadis*) are used for many purposes and are the invariable accompaniments of these temples.

Besides these, a south Indian temple usually has a tank called the *Kalyani* or *Pushkarni* – to be used for sacred purposes or the convenience of the priests – dwellings for all the grades of the priesthood are attached to it, and other buildings for state or convenience.[[118]](#cite_note-Fergusson-120)

### Theatre, dance and music

Main articles: [Carnatic music](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music"), [Ancient Tamil music](/wiki/Ancient_Tamil_music "Ancient Tamil music"), [Music of Kerala](/wiki/Music_of_Kerala "Music of Kerala"), [Music of Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Music_of_Tamil_Nadu "Music of Tamil Nadu"), [Music of Andhra Pradesh](/wiki/Music_of_Andhra_Pradesh "Music of Andhra Pradesh"), and [Cinema of South India](/wiki/Cinema_of_South_India "Cinema of South India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Kuchipudi1.jpg/250px-Kuchipudi1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kuchipudi1.jpg)

A [Kuchipudi](/wiki/Kuchipudi "Kuchipudi") dancer.

Literary evidence of traditional form of theatre, dance and music dates back to the 3rd century BCE.[[119]](#cite_note-Nijenhuis_1974-121) Ancient literary works, such as the *[Cilappatikaram](/wiki/Cilappatikaram "Cilappatikaram")*, describe a [system of music](/wiki/Ancient_Tamil_music "Ancient Tamil music").[[119]](#cite_note-Nijenhuis_1974-121) The theatrical culture flourished during the early Sangam age. Theatre-dance traditions have a long and varied history whose origins can be traced back almost two millennia to dance-theatre forms like *Kotukotti*, Kaapaalam and *Pandarangam*, which are mentioned in an ancient anthology of poems entitled the [Kaliththokai](/wiki/Kaliththokai "Kaliththokai").[[120]](#cite_note-122) Dance forms such as [Bharatanatyam](/wiki/Bharatanatyam "Bharatanatyam") are based on older temple dance forms known as *Catir Kacceri*, as practised by [courtesans](/wiki/Courtesans "Courtesans") and a class of women known as *[Devadasis](/wiki/Devadasis "Devadasis")*.[[121]](#cite_note-Leslie-123)

[Carnatic music](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") originated in the Dravidian region. With the growing influence of Persian and Sufi music on Indian music, a clear distinction in style appeared from the 12th century onwards. Many literary works were composed in Carnatic style and it soon spread wide in the Dravidian regions. The most notable Carnatic musician is [Purandara Dasa](/wiki/Purandara_Dasa "Purandara Dasa") who lived in the court of [Krishnadevaraya](/wiki/Krishnadevaraya "Krishnadevaraya") of the [Vijayanagara](/wiki/Vijayanagara "Vijayanagara") empire. He formulated the basic structure of Carnatic music and is regarded as the *Pitamaha* (*lit*, "father" or the "grandfather") of Carnatic Music. [Kanakadasa](/wiki/Kanakadasa "Kanakadasa") is another notable Carnatic musician who was Purandaradasa's contemporary.

Each of the major Dravidian languages has its own film industry like [Kollywood](/wiki/Kollywood "Kollywood") (Tamil), [Tollywood](/wiki/Tollywood_(Telugu) "Tollywood (Telugu)") (Telugu), [Sandalwood](/wiki/Kannada_cinema "Kannada cinema") (Kannada), [Mollywood](/wiki/Malayalam_cinema "Malayalam cinema") (Malayalam). Kollywood and Tollywood produce most films in India.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

### Clothing

Main articles: [Lungi](/wiki/Lungi "Lungi"), [Sari](/wiki/Sari "Sari"), and [Dhoti](/wiki/Dhoti "Dhoti")

Dravidian speakers in southern India wear varied traditional costumes depending on their region, largely influenced by local customs and traditions. The most traditional dress for Dravidian men is the [lungi](/wiki/Lungi "Lungi"), or the more formal [dhoti](/wiki/Dhoti "Dhoti"), called *veshti* in Tamil, *panche* in Kannada and Telugu, and *mundu* in Malayalam. The lungi consists of a colourful checked cotton cloth. Many times these lungis are tube-shaped and tied around the waist, and can be easily tied above the knees for more strenuous activities. The lungi is usually everyday dress, used for doing labour while dhoti is used for more formal occasions. Many villagers have only a lungi as their article of clothing. The dhoti is generally white in colour, and occasionally has a border of red, green or gold. Dhotis are usually made out of cotton for more everyday use, but the more expensive silk dhotis are used for special functions like festivals and weddings.

Traditional dress of Dravidian women is typical of most Indian women, that of the sari. This sari consists of a cloth wrapped around the waist and draped over the shoulder. Originally saris were worn bare, but during the Victorian era, women began wearing blouse (called a ravike) along with sari. In fact, until the late 19th century most Kerala women did not wear any upper garments, or were forced to by law, and in many villages, especially in tribal communities, the sari is worn without the blouse. Unlike Indo-Aryan speakers, most Dravidian women do not cover their head with the pallu except in areas of North Karnataka. Due to the complexity of draping the sari, younger girls start with a skirt called a *pavada*. When they get older, around the age when puberty begins, they transition to a *[langa voni](/wiki/Langa_voni "Langa voni")* or half-sari, which is composed of a skirt tied at the waist along with a cloth draped over a blouse. After adulthood girls begin using the sari. There are many different styles of sari draping varying across regions and communities. Examples are the [Madisar](/wiki/Madisar "Madisar"), specific to Tamil Brahmin Community, and the [Mundum Neriyathum](/wiki/Mundum_Neriyathum "Mundum Neriyathum").

### Martial arts and sports

Main articles: [Kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu"), [Kuttu Varisai](/wiki/Kuttu_Varisai "Kuttu Varisai"), [Varma Kalai](/wiki/Varma_Kalai "Varma Kalai"), [Silambam](/wiki/Silambam "Silambam"), [Adithada](/wiki/Adithada "Adithada"), [Malyutham](/wiki/Malyutham "Malyutham"), and [Traditional games of India](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_India "Traditional games of India")

In [Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata"), [Bhishma](/wiki/Bhishma "Bhishma") claimed that southerners are skilled with sword-fighting in general and [Sahadeva](/wiki/Sahadeva "Sahadeva") was chosen for the conquest of the southern kingdoms due to his swordsmanship.[[123]](#cite_note-125) In South India various types of martial arts are practised like [Kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu") and [Silambam](/wiki/Silambam "Silambam").

In ancient times there were *[ankams](/wiki/Ankam "Ankam")*, public duels to the death, to solve disputes between opposing rulers.[[124]](#cite_note-Zarrilli_1998-126) Among some communities, young girls received preliminary training up until the onset of puberty.[[124]](#cite_note-Zarrilli_1998-126) In *vadakkan pattukal* ballads, at least a few women warriors continued to practice and achieved a high degree of expertise.[[124]](#cite_note-Zarrilli_1998-126)

Sports like [kambala](/wiki/Kambala "Kambala"), [jallikattu](/wiki/Jallikattu "Jallikattu"), [kabaddi](/wiki/Kabaddi "Kabaddi"), [vallam kali](/wiki/Vallam_kali "Vallam kali"), [lambs and tigers](/wiki/Lambs_and_tigers "Lambs and tigers"), and [maramadi](/wiki/Maramadi "Maramadi") remain strong among Dravidian ethnic groups.
